events.text: The patient is a 66-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is of West African descent, a retired high school science teacher, nonsmoker, with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 10 years prior, on metformin), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. No family history of pancreatic cancer. BMI 24.1 kg/m2.

At age 66, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive epigastric pain radiating to the back, new-onset weight loss of approximately 14 lbs, and new-onset diabetes worsening (previously well-controlled on metformin, now with fasting glucoses in the 220-280 mg/dL range despite medication adjustment). She also reported early satiety and intermittent nausea. Physical exam revealed mild epigastric tenderness without jaundice. Labs notable for CA 19-9 of 1,200 U/mL, CEA 5.8 ng/mL, total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 142 U/mL, ALT 54 U/mL, AST 48 U/mL. HbA1c 9.2%. She was referred urgently for cross-sectional imaging.

At age 66, the patient underwent a CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (triphasic with thin cuts). Findings: a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process with poorly defined margins. Tumor contacts greater than 180 degrees of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over a length of approximately 1.8 cm. Tumor abuts the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) with narrowing of the SMV lumen by approximately 40% over a 2.1 cm segment; no occlusion. No involvement of the portal vein confluence. No involvement of the celiac axis or hepatic artery. No radiographically apparent lymph node metastases (nodes measuring up to 9 mm). No liver lesions or peritoneal implants detected. No ascites. Impression: Borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma by NCCN criteria (SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV abutment with lumen narrowing). Cancer present. No distant metastasis identified.

At age 66, the patient underwent a PET-CT (18F-FDG) for staging purposes. Findings: hypermetabolic activity in the pancreatic head/uncinate mass (SUVmax 9.2) consistent with the known primary tumor. No FDG-avid hepatic lesions. No FDG-avid peritoneal deposits. No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid lymph nodes in the peripancreatic, mesenteric, or retroperitoneal regions. No distant metastatic disease identified. Impression: Locally advanced disease without evidence of distant metastasis. Cancer present.

At age 66, the patient underwent an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS)-guided fine needle biopsy (FNB) of the pancreatic head mass. Pathology report: Specimen type: EUS-FNB, pancreatic head. Diagnosis: ADENOCARCINOMA, PANCREATIC DUCTAL TYPE (MALIGNANT, PRIMARY). Microscopic description: Atypical glandular structures embedded in a desmoplastic stroma with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, irregular nuclear contours, prominent nucleoli, and infiltrative growth pattern. Mucin production present. Features consistent with moderately differentiated (grade 2) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK19 positive, MUC1 positive, MUC5AC focally positive, CDX2 negative, synaptophysin negative, chromogranin negative, p53 aberrant (strong diffuse overexpression pattern). Smad4/DPC4 retained (positive) staining. Ki-67 proliferative index approximately 30%.

At age 66, the patient received a diagnosis of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process. AJCC 8th Edition Clinical Stage: T3 N0 M0, Stage IIA (by clinical staging based on cross-sectional imaging). Summary Stage: Regional (borderline resectable by NCCN criteria due to SMA abutment >180 degrees and SMV abutment with lumen narrowing). Primary site: Pancreatic head/uncinate process (ICD-O-3 site code C25.0). Histology: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated (ICD-O-3 histology code 8500/3). Tumor size: 3.4 cm. Relevant biomarkers: CA 19-9 1,200 U/mL at diagnosis; CEA 5.8 ng/mL. Smad4/DPC4 retained. p53 aberrant overexpression. Grade: G2 (moderately differentiated). Confirmation: Positive histology via EUS-FNB. Laterality: Midline/head of pancreas.

At age 66, the patient underwent next-generation sequencing (Tempus xT solid tumor panel) performed on tissue from the EUS-FNB specimen of the pancreatic head mass. Results: Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 3 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: Microsatellite stable (MSS). Actionable/clinically relevant alterations: KRAS p.G12V (variant allele frequency 42%, pathogenic, oncogenic driver, no approved targeted therapy; RAS inhibitors in development). TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%, pathogenic, loss-of-function, consistent with IHC overexpression pattern). CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss, tumor suppressor, no targeted therapy). SMAD4 wild-type (consistent with IHC). Additional findings: ARID1A p.Q1368* (VAF 11%, likely pathogenic, chromatin remodeling). RNF43 p.G659Vfs*41 (VAF 28%, pathogenic, WNT pathway). KDM6A deep deletion (copy number loss). ATM wild-type. BRCA1 wild-type. BRCA2 wild-type. PALB2 wild-type. FGFR2 wild-type. No NTRK fusions detected. No ALK/ROS1 fusions detected. No NRG1 fusions detected. No actionable fusions or gene amplifications identified. Germline testing recommended given pancreatic cancer diagnosis; patient subsequently had germline testing which showed no pathogenic germline variants in BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, or MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2.

At age 66, the patient was enrolled in a multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board discussion and the consensus recommendation was neoadjuvant chemotherapy with modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) for borderline resectable disease with the intent of tumor downstaging, followed by restaging and consideration for chemoradiation and then surgical resection. Cycle 1 of mFOLFIRINOX was initiated: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 IV day 1 (reduced from 180 mg/m2 given patient age and diabetes), 5-fluorouracil 2,400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours; every 2 weeks. Supportive medications: granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) filgrastim prophylaxis, ondansetron, prochlorperazine, and loperamide as needed. Intent: neoadjuvant/curative.

At age 66, during cycles 2-4 of mFOLFIRINOX, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (paresthesias in fingertips and toes, worsened by cold exposure), grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 nausea (partially controlled with antiemetics), and grade 1 alopecia. She also developed grade 2 diarrhea during cycle 3, managed with loperamide and dose reduction of irinotecan to 120 mg/m2 for subsequent cycles. No febrile neutropenia. No hospitalizations required. ECOG performance status remained 1 throughout.

At age 66, after 4 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (approximately 2 months into treatment), the patient underwent restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol. Findings: The pancreatic head/uncinate mass measures 3.0 cm (previously 3.4 cm), representing a minor decrease in size. Persistence of SMA abutment, now measuring approximately 160 degrees of contact (previously >180 degrees), slight improvement. SMV narrowing persists but appears slightly less severe. No new liver lesions. No new peritoneal deposits. No new lymphadenopathy. CA 19-9 at this restaging: 380 U/mL (down from 1,200 U/mL at baseline). Impression: Mild partial radiographic response per modified RECIST criteria. Cancer present. Responding to therapy. Recommendation: Continue mFOLFIRINOX toward planned 8 cycles.

At age 66, at the midpoint restaging visit after 4 cycles, the oncologist noted continued CA 19-9 decline (1,200 to 380 U/mL), mild radiographic response, and improvement in back pain. Patient's weight had stabilized. Nausea and fatigue were the predominant side effects. The plan was to continue mFOLFIRINOX for an additional 4 cycles (cycles 5-8), then perform definitive restaging. Performance status ECOG 1. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, after completing 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX (approximately 4 months after treatment initiation), the patient underwent definitive restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol and MRI liver. CT findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measures 2.6 cm (previously 3.0 cm at interim restaging, 3.4 cm at baseline), representing a partial response by RECIST 1.1 (>30% reduction in longest diameter from baseline). SMA abutment has decreased to approximately 120 degrees of contact (previously >180 degrees at baseline). SMV lumen narrowing has improved with now only mild abutment without significant luminal compromise. MRI liver: No hepatic metastases identified. No peritoneal implants. No new lymphadenopathy. Impression: Partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX. Improved but persistent SMA abutment (120 degrees). Improved SMV relationship. Case to be re-presented at multidisciplinary tumor board. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, following completion of 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX, CA 19-9 declined to 85 U/mL (from 1,200 U/mL at baseline; 93% reduction). Patient re-presented at multidisciplinary pancreatic tumor board. Surgical oncologist noted improved but persistent SMA abutment at 120 degrees, which remained technically borderline. Consensus decision: proceed with chemoradiation (capecitabine-based long-course) prior to surgical exploration, with the goal of further local consolidation and improving the likelihood of R0 resection. Patient counseled on the plan. ECOG performance status 1. No evidence of distant metastasis. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, approximately 4.5 months after diagnosis, the patient received concurrent chemoradiation therapy (long-course). Radiation: stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT)-based approach was considered but conventional long-course selected given degree of vascular abutment. Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) delivered via intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to the pancreatic head tumor with a 5 mm clinical target volume margin. Concurrent chemotherapy: capecitabine 825 mg/m2 orally twice daily on radiation days. Radiation field included primary tumor with margin; prophylactic nodal irradiation was not performed. Treatment completed over approximately 5.5 weeks without treatment breaks. Intent: neoadjuvant/curative, local consolidation prior to surgery.

At age 66, during chemoradiation, the patient experienced grade 2 nausea and vomiting (managed with ondansetron and metoclopramide), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 radiation dermatitis overlying the epigastric region, and grade 1 diarrhea (managed conservatively). She required a 3-day treatment break in week 4 due to grade 2 nausea with poor oral intake. Blood glucose remained elevated during radiation, requiring insulin dose adjustment. No grade 3 or higher toxicities. Weight loss of 4 lbs during chemoradiation. ECOG performance status 1-2 during treatment, returning to 1 by end of chemoradiation.

At age 66, approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation, the patient underwent a restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol prior to surgical planning. Findings: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass measures 2.3 cm (previously 2.6 cm after mFOLFIRINOX). Persistent soft tissue density in the region consistent with treated tumor versus fibrosis, difficult to distinguish post-treatment changes. SMA abutment now estimated at 80-90 degrees of contact. SMV relationship: abutment without significant luminal narrowing. No liver metastases. No peritoneal deposits. No lymphadenopathy. CA 19-9 at this time: 52 U/mL. Impression: Continued response to neoadjuvant therapy. Reduced SMA abutment to <90 degrees. Potentially resectable. Surgical oncology consultation for Whipple procedure planning. Cancer present, responding to therapy.

At age 66, approximately 8 weeks after completing chemoradiation (approximately 7 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent exploratory laparotomy and pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) performed by an experienced hepatopancreaticobiliary (HPB) surgical oncologist at a high-volume center. Operative findings: No peritoneal metastases. No liver surface metastases. Frozen section of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) margin: negative for malignancy. Intraoperative ultrasound confirmed no liver lesions. The procedure included: pancreaticoduodenectomy with pylorus-preserving technique, regional lymphadenectomy (stations 5, 6, 8a, 12b, 13, 14, 17), portal vein/SMV reconstruction was not required as the vessel was freed with careful dissection. Total operative time: 6 hours 45 minutes. Estimated blood loss: 450 mL. No intraoperative transfusions required. Patient transferred to surgical ICU postoperatively in stable condition.

At age 66, final surgical pathology from the Whipple (pancreaticoduodenectomy) specimen was reported. Specimen: Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy. Diagnosis: PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (Grade 2). Tumor location: Pancreatic head/uncinate process. Tumor size: 2.1 cm (greatest dimension; reduced from 3.4 cm at diagnosis, representing significant treatment response). Histologic treatment response (College of American Pathologists grading): Grade 2 (moderate response; residual cancer with evident tumor regression, estimated 40% viable tumor cells remaining, 60% fibrosis/acellular mucin pools). Margins: Surgical margins negative for invasive carcinoma (R0 resection). Margin distances: Posterior/retroperitoneal margin: 2 mm. Uncinate/SMA margin: 3 mm (closest margin). Anterior surface: 5 mm. Bile duct margin: negative (>10 mm). Pancreatic neck margin: negative (>10 mm). Duodenal margins (proximal and distal): negative. Lymph nodes: 2 of 18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (2/18; pN1). Largest metastatic deposit: 4 mm. No extranodal extension. Lymphovascular invasion: Present. Perineural invasion: Present (extensive). Pathologic staging (AJCC 8th Edition): pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB. SMAD4/DPC4 immunohistochemistry on resection specimen: retained (consistent with prior biopsy).

At age 66, at the post-operative visit 4 weeks after Whipple procedure, the patient was recovering from surgery. She developed a grade B pancreatic fistula (International Study Group of Pancreatic Fistula [ISGPF] grade B) on postoperative day 6, as evidenced by elevated amylase in drain fluid (drain fluid amylase >3x upper limit of normal on postoperative day 3). This was managed conservatively with continued surgical drain in place, NPO (nothing by mouth), total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneously three times daily. The drain was kept in place for 6 weeks until drain output decreased and amylase normalized. No surgical re-intervention was required. Additional postoperative complications: delayed gastric emptying (grade A, resolved by postoperative day 14 with nasogastric tube decompression). The patient was discharged home on postoperative day 18 with drain in place. Pathologic staging: pT3 pN1 M0 (Stage IIB). R0 resection confirmed. Cancer: post-resection status, no gross residual disease.

At age 66, approximately 10 weeks after the Whipple procedure (after pancreatic fistula resolved and prior to initiating adjuvant chemotherapy), the patient underwent CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast for baseline post-surgical imaging. Findings: Post-pancreaticoduodenectomy changes with expected anatomical alterations (hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy, gastrojejunostomy). No fluid collections or abscesses. Surgical drain has been removed (externally). No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No lymphadenopathy meeting criteria for malignancy. CA 19-9: 28 U/mL (normalized; upper limit of normal 37 U/mL). CEA: 2.1 ng/mL. Impression: Expected post-surgical changes without evidence of residual or recurrent disease. No cancer identified on this study.

At age 66, approximately 12 weeks after the Whipple procedure (following resolution of the grade B pancreatic fistula and recovery), the patient initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine plus capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen): gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle plus capecitabine 1,660 mg/m2/day (830 mg/m2 twice daily) orally on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle. Total planned: 6 cycles (approximately 6 months). ECOG performance status 1. Intent: adjuvant/curative.

At age 66, during adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine, the patient experienced the following toxicities: Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) beginning cycle 2, managed with dose reduction of capecitabine to 75% (1,245 mg/m2/day) after cycle 2. Grade 2 fatigue throughout treatment. Grade 1 myelosuppression (mild anemia; nadir hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, managed with iron supplementation). Grade 1 nausea. Day 8 gemcitabine omitted in cycles 3 and 5 due to neutropenia (ANC <1.0 x 10^9/L). Re-hospitalization on cycle 3 day 12 for a suspected pancreatic fistula recurrence: the patient presented with abdominal pain and elevated serum amylase/lipase. CT abdomen confirmed a small peripancreatic fluid collection (4 cm); managed conservatively with IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam), bowel rest, and TPN. No percutaneous drainage required. Collection resolved within 2 weeks. Chemotherapy held for 3 weeks during this hospitalization, then resumed with capecitabine dose reduction to 50% for cycle 4. Despite dose modifications and treatment delays, all 6 cycles were ultimately completed over approximately 9 months (rather than the planned 6 months).

At age 66 (during adjuvant therapy), the oncologist noted that the patient was tolerating adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine with significant dose modifications and delays secondary to recurrent pancreatic fistula and hand-foot syndrome. Despite these complications, she was able to complete all 6 planned cycles. CA 19-9 remained within normal range throughout adjuvant therapy (nadir 18 U/mL). ECOG performance status 1. Patient continued on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT; pancrelipase) and insulin for post-pancreatectomy exocrine and endocrine insufficiency. No evidence of disease recurrence. Cancer absent.

At age 67, approximately 3 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy (approximately 18 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: No new hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No pulmonary nodules or masses suspicious for metastasis. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. Post-surgical anatomy unchanged from prior study. CA 19-9: 22 U/mL (within normal limits). CEA: 2.4 ng/mL. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Cancer absent.

At age 67, approximately 6 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy (approximately 21 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No pulmonary metastases. Post-surgical anatomy stable. Mild peripancreatic fibrosis at the anastomotic site, unchanged. CA 19-9: 19 U/mL. CEA: 2.2 ng/mL. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Cancer absent.

At age 67, approximately 12 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy (approximately 27 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Findings: New 1.2 cm hypodense hepatic lesion in segment VI, indeterminate but suspicious given oncologic history. Additionally, two new small indeterminate pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 7 mm and left lower lobe 5 mm (previously absent). No peritoneal implants. CA 19-9: 67 U/mL (rising above upper limit of normal of 37 U/mL). CEA: 4.8 ng/mL (rising). Impression: New indeterminate hepatic lesion and pulmonary nodules in the context of rising CA 19-9 and CEA, highly suspicious for metastatic recurrence. Further characterization recommended. Cancer present (suspected).

At age 67, following the suspicious surveillance CT, the patient underwent MRI liver with gadoxetate disodium (Eovist) contrast for further characterization of the hepatic lesion. Findings: The 1.2 cm segment VI hepatic lesion demonstrates arterial phase enhancement with portal venous washout and hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase images. Features are highly characteristic of metastatic adenocarcinoma rather than hepatocellular carcinoma. No additional hepatic lesions identified. Impression: Hepatic segment VI lesion consistent with metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Cancer present.

At age 67, the patient underwent CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the segment VI hepatic lesion. Pathology: Specimen: Core needle biopsy, liver, segment VI. Diagnosis: METASTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH PANCREATIC PRIMARY (MALIGNANT). Microscopic description: Infiltrative glandular structures with desmoplastic stroma, moderate-to-severe nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and intraluminal mucin, identical in morphology to the prior pancreatic primary. Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK19 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, TTF-1 negative, PAX8 negative, MUC1 positive, SMAD4/DPC4 retained. Findings confirm metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

At age 67, the patient was presented at multidisciplinary tumor board following biopsy-confirmed hepatic metastasis (segment VI), two indeterminate pulmonary nodules (likely metastatic given the hepatic confirmation), and rising CA 19-9 (67 U/mL) and CEA (4.8 ng/mL). Pathologic stage now M1 (Stage IV metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma). The patient had recurred approximately 12 months after completing adjuvant therapy (approximately 27 months from initial diagnosis). Disease-free interval: approximately 20 months from surgery. ECOG performance status 1. Prior therapies: mFOLFIRINOX (8 cycles neoadjuvant), capecitabine-based chemoradiation, gemcitabine/capecitabine (6 cycles adjuvant). Molecular profile reviewed: KRAS G12V mutant, MSS, TMB-low, SMAD4 retained, no actionable fusions, no BRCA/ATM/PALB2 mutations. Plan: systemic therapy for metastatic disease. Given prior FOLFIRINOX exposure and >12 month interval from last chemotherapy, discussed options including gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, re-challenge with FOLFIRINOX, or clinical trial enrollment. Patient elected to pursue gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel given prior oxaliplatin-associated neuropathy and convenience. Cancer present, disease progression (new metastatic recurrence).

At age 67, the patient initiated first-line palliative systemic therapy for metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma with gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m2 IV plus nab-paclitaxel 125 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle. Concurrent supportive care: ondansetron, dexamethasone premedication for nab-paclitaxel, G-CSF per institutional protocol. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy and insulin continued. Palliative care consultation initiated. Intent: palliative.

At age 67, during palliative gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, the patient developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (additive to residual oxaliplatin-associated neuropathy from prior FOLFIRINOX), grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 alopecia, and grade 2 neutropenia requiring dose reduction of nab-paclitaxel to 100 mg/m2 after cycle 2. Day 8 and day 15 doses were held once each in cycles 3 and 4 due to neutropenia. No febrile neutropenia. Grade 1 nausea throughout. Blood glucose remained elevated, requiring insulin dose adjustments.

At age 67, after 3 cycles of gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, the patient underwent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Findings: The hepatic segment VI lesion is stable at 1.2 cm. Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule stable at 7 mm; left lower lobe nodule stable at 5 mm. No new lesions. No peritoneal implants. CA 19-9: 45 U/mL (declining from 67 U/mL). CEA: 3.9 ng/mL. Impression: Stable disease by RECIST 1.1. CA 19-9 trending down. Cancer present. Responding (stable disease) to therapy.

At age 67, following 3 cycles of gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel with stable disease and declining CA 19-9, the oncology team discussed continuation of therapy. The patient expressed goals of care focused on maintaining quality of life and functional independence. She remained ECOG performance status 1 with manageable toxicities. Plan: continue gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel with dose modifications as needed. Palliative care team co-managing symptom burden. Advance directives updated. Cancer present, stable disease on palliative therapy.

documents.text:
DEPARTMENT OF INTERNAL MEDICINE / PRIMARY CARE
OFFICE VISIT NOTE — URGENT EVALUATION

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
AGE: 66 years
SEX: Female
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Primary Care Physician]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African woman who presents today with a 3-month history of progressive epigastric pain radiating to the back, unintentional weight loss of approximately 14 pounds, and worsening blood glucose control in the setting of previously well-managed type 2 diabetes. She was seen at an urgent care visit last week and referred here for urgent evaluation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old retired high school science teacher with known type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia who presents for urgent evaluation of a 3-month progressive illness characterized by the following constellation of symptoms:

Epigastric pain: The patient describes a constant, dull, occasionally sharp aching discomfort in the mid-epigastric region that she initially attributed to indigestion or her diabetes medications. Over the past 6-8 weeks, the pain has progressively intensified and now radiates to the mid-back, typically worse after eating. She rates the pain as a 4-5/10 at baseline, worsening to 7/10 post-prandially and in the supine position. She notes some relief when leaning forward or sitting upright. She denies frank hematemesis or melena, although she reports her stools have appeared lighter in color over the past month (described as "tan or clay-colored" on two to three occasions). She has not noted bright red blood per rectum.

Weight loss: The patient reports unintentional weight loss of approximately 14 lbs over the past 2.5 to 3 months. She states she has had diminished appetite throughout this period, with significant early satiety — often feeling full after only a few bites of food. She has not made any intentional dietary changes. Prior to this illness, her weight had been stable at approximately 145 lbs for several years.

Worsening diabetes: Her type 2 diabetes, previously well-controlled on metformin 1,000 mg twice daily with fasting glucoses consistently in the 90-130 mg/dL range over the past several years, has become markedly unstable. Over the past 6 weeks, fasting glucose values have been running in the 220-280 mg/dL range despite no dietary changes and her medication regimen. Her primary care nurse practitioner increased her metformin dose approximately 3 weeks ago without significant improvement. There is no prior history of insulin dependence. She has not had prior episodes of glucose instability of this magnitude.

Nausea: She reports intermittent nausea, non-bloody, non-bilious, occurring most days but especially after meals. She has had approximately 3-4 episodes of emesis over the past month. No hematemesis.

Jaundice/skin changes: She denies frank jaundice or scleral icterus. She has not noticed yellowing of her skin. She denies pruritus. No dark urine consistently, though she noted her urine appeared somewhat amber-colored on a few occasions.

New-onset diabetes consideration: Of particular concern, the acute destabilization of her previously controlled diabetes in the setting of epigastric pain, back pain, and weight loss raises the concern for new-onset or worsening pancreatic disease. She had no prior episodes of pancreatitis. No history of heavy alcohol use (drinks rarely, less than 2 drinks per year by her account). No history of gallstones or cholelithiasis by any prior imaging.

Fatigue: She reports significant fatigue over the past 2 months, which she attributes in part to poor sleep (secondary to back pain) and poor appetite. She has reduced her activity level noticeably.

No fevers or chills. No rigors. No prior episodes of pancreatitis. No prior abdominal surgeries. No history of inflammatory bowel disease. No recent travel outside the country. No sick contacts.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Positive for unintentional weight loss (~14 lbs over 3 months), fatigue, decreased appetite, early satiety. No fever, no chills, no night sweats.
Gastrointestinal: Positive for epigastric pain radiating to the back, intermittent nausea and vomiting (3-4 episodes), early satiety, pale/clay-colored stools on several occasions, mild amber-tinged urine on occasion. Negative for hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, frank jaundice. No dysphagia, odynophagia, or change in bowel habit otherwise.
Musculoskeletal: Positive for mid-back pain radiating from the epigastric region. Denies new joint pain or swelling.
Neurologic: Denies headache, focal weakness, sensory changes, or confusion.
Endocrine: Positive for deterioration in glycemic control as described. No new polyuria beyond what she has been experiencing due to hyperglycemia.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Denies cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Genitourinary: Denies dysuria, hematuria. Occasional amber urine noted as above.
Skin: Denies pruritus. No jaundice noted by patient.
Psychiatric: Positive for significant anxiety about her symptoms. No depression formally screened yet but patient expresses worry about her health and what these symptoms might mean.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 10 years ago; previously well-controlled on metformin. Current HbA1c (today's labs): 9.2% (see below). Prior HbA1c at last check 14 months ago was 6.8%.
2. Hypertension — on lisinopril 10 mg daily; well-controlled.
3. Hyperlipidemia — on atorvastatin 40 mg daily; last LDL 78 mg/dL at annual visit.
4. No prior history of pancreatitis, gallstones, or biliary disease.
5. No prior malignancy.
6. No prior abdominal surgeries.
7. Colonoscopy 3 years ago: normal, no polyps.
8. Mammography last performed 2 years ago: normal.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
No prior surgeries.

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother: Died of stroke at age 78; had type 2 diabetes and hypertension.
Father: Died of cardiac disease at age 71.
Sibling (brother): Type 2 diabetes, alive.
No family history of pancreatic cancer, colorectal cancer, ovarian cancer, breast cancer, or other known hereditary cancer syndromes. No family members with jaundice or biliary disease that she is aware of. No family history of BRCA-associated or Lynch syndrome-associated cancers.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Occupation: Retired high school science teacher (retired 2 years ago).
Residence: Lives with husband; two adult children.
Tobacco: Nonsmoker; never smoked.
Alcohol: Rare to none; patient reports less than 2 alcoholic drinks per year.
Illicit substances: None.
Exercise: Was walking 30 minutes 4-5 days per week prior to symptom onset; now significantly reduced due to fatigue and pain.
Diet: Follows a low-carbohydrate diet for diabetes management. No recent dietary changes.
Health literacy: High; patient is well-educated, engaged, and asks detailed questions about her care.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies (NKDA).

MEDICATIONS
1. Metformin 1,000 mg orally twice daily (with meals) — type 2 diabetes mellitus; recently increased dose per nurse practitioner with inadequate response
2. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily — hypertension
3. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily (at bedtime) — hyperlipidemia
4. Low-dose aspirin 81 mg orally once daily — cardiovascular prophylaxis
5. Calcium carbonate with vitamin D 500 mg/400 IU orally once daily — supplementation

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs:
  Temperature: 98.2°F (36.8°C)
  Blood Pressure: 138/84 mmHg (mildly elevated above her usual baseline of 120s/70s)
  Heart Rate: 82 beats per minute, regular
  Respiratory Rate: 16 breaths per minute
  Oxygen Saturation: 98% on room air
  Weight: 131 lbs (BMI 22.4 kg/m2; per chart, baseline weight approximately 145 lbs ~3 months ago — weight loss of ~14 lbs confirmed)
  Pain Score: 4/10

General: Well-appearing West African woman who appears mildly uncomfortable. Alert and oriented x3. No acute distress. She appears mildly fatigued. She is cooperative and engaged.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric — no scleral icterus appreciated on careful exam. Conjunctivae pink, no pallor. Oropharynx clear and moist. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Effort unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Active bowel sounds in all four quadrants. MILD epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, without guarding or rigidity. No rebound tenderness. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman presenting with a highly concerning clinical picture: a 3-month history of progressive epigastric and back pain, unintentional weight loss of 14 lbs, new-onset dramatic worsening of previously well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c rising from 6.8% to 9.2% in just over a year with acute glucose destabilization in the 220-280 mg/dL range), mildly elevated liver enzymes with borderline total bilirubin, and a CA 19-9 of 1,200 U/mL — a level markedly elevated above normal and highly concerning for a pancreatic malignancy, most likely pancreatic adenocarcinoma until proven otherwise.

Differential Diagnosis:
The constellation of symptoms, signs, and markedly elevated CA 19-9 is highly suspicious for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The differential also includes:
- Autoimmune pancreatitis (IgG4-related) — would consider in the absence of a mass; less likely given degree of CA 19-9 elevation
- Chronic pancreatitis with pseudocyst — less likely given no prior pancreatitis and minimal lipase elevation
- Primary biliary pathology (bile duct obstruction, cholangiocarcinoma) — the mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase and borderline bilirubin raise this; CT imaging will be critical
- Ampullary or duodenal adenocarcinoma — possible, CT will help differentiate
- Gastric carcinoma — less consistent with the back pain pattern and CA 19-9 elevation
- Metastatic disease with pancreatic involvement — less likely as primary presentation without known primary

However, this presentation must be approached as pancreatic malignancy until proven otherwise. The acute worsening of diabetes in the setting of abdominal pain, weight loss, and markedly elevated CA 19-9 is a classic presentation of pancreatic head adenocarcinoma.

1. URGENT IMAGING: CT abdomen/pelvis with dedicated pancreatic protocol (triphasic, thin cuts) ordered STAT. If a pancreatic mass is identified, this will be followed by pancreatic protocol MRI and/or EUS-guided biopsy as indicated. The patient has been given the order and instructed to present to radiology within 24-48 hours.

2. ONCOLOGY/GASTROENTEROLOGY REFERRAL: Urgent referral placed to both medical oncology and gastroenterology (for EUS consideration) today given the highly suspicious clinical picture. The referral coordinator has been alerted to expedite appointments.

3. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: Given the acute glucose instability — fasting glucoses running 220-280 mg/dL despite maximal metformin — and given that pancreatic pathology may be contributing directly (pancreatic endocrine dysfunction in the setting of a mass), I am adding a short-acting insulin correction scale and starting basal insulin glargine 10 units subcutaneously at bedtime, to be titrated. Metformin will be continued for now but may need to be held depending on CT contrast use and renal function. Patient educated on insulin administration and hypoglycemia precautions. Glucagon kit prescribed.

4. NUTRITION: Given significant weight loss (14 lbs over 3 months) and early satiety, the patient is at risk for nutritional compromise. Dietary counseling referral placed. High-protein, calorie-dense nutritional supplementation (e.g., Ensure, Boost) encouraged pending further workup.

5. PAIN MANAGEMENT: For back and epigastric pain management in the near term, prescribing acetaminophen 500-1000 mg every 6-8 hours as needed. Given the possibility of a pancreatic diagnosis and concern for opioid constipation complicating the workup, avoiding narcotics at this time unless necessary. Recommend NSAIDs with caution given renal considerations and aspirin use.

6. MONITORING: Repeat LFTs and bilirubin in 1-2 weeks or sooner depending on imaging findings, given the borderline bilirubinemia — if a pancreatic head mass with biliary obstruction is identified, hepatobiliary surgery and ERCP/stenting may be needed urgently.

7. COUNSELING: The patient and her husband were present for today's visit. I had a frank conversation about the concerning nature of these findings, particularly the markedly elevated CA 19-9 and the clinical picture, and expressed that urgent evaluation for a possible pancreatic process is necessary. I was honest but compassionate in explaining that while CA 19-9 can be elevated in benign conditions, the level here (1,200 U/mL) combined with her symptoms warrants very expedited evaluation. I emphasized that we do not have a diagnosis yet and that imaging is the next critical step. The patient is a retired science teacher, understands the concern, and wishes to proceed with all recommended testing as quickly as possible. She is emotionally appropriate — concerned but composed — and expressed willingness to pursue all available evaluation. I have given her contact information for urgent triage should she develop worsening pain, jaundice, fever, or inability to eat or drink before the CT scan.

CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC) AND MRI LIVER WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 66-year-old female with known borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V mutant, moderately differentiated, pT3 N0 at clinical staging). Presenting for definitive restaging following completion of 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Baseline tumor measured 3.4 cm with >180 degrees SMA abutment and SMV lumen narrowing at approximately 40%. Prior 4-cycle interim restaging (approximately 2 months ago) demonstrated a modest decrease to 3.0 cm with improvement in SMA contact to approximately 160 degrees. CA 19-9 at this visit: 85 U/mL (baseline 1,200 U/mL; prior restaging 380 U/mL).

TECHNIQUE:

CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Pancreatic Protocol: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed following administration of intravenous iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350, 120 mL) with triphasic acquisition: non-contrast, late arterial/pancreatic parenchymal phase (approximately 40 seconds post-injection), and portal venous phase (approximately 70 seconds post-injection). Thin-section axial images were acquired at 0.625 mm collimation with multiplanar reformats in the coronal and sagittal planes. Curved planar reformatted images were generated along the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and superior mesenteric vein (SMV)/portal vein axis for vascular evaluation. Maximum intensity projection (MIP) images were reviewed for vascular anatomy.

MRI Liver: Dedicated liver MRI performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet with hepatocyte-specific gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadoxetate disodium, Eovist/Primovist, 0.025 mmol/kg IV). Sequences acquired: T2-weighted fast spin echo (axial and coronal), diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI, b-values 0, 400, 800 s/mm2) with corresponding ADC maps, T1 in-phase and opposed-phase, dynamic contrast-enhanced T1-weighted fat-suppressed sequences (pre-contrast, arterial, portal venous, delayed at 3 minutes, and hepatobiliary phase at 20 minutes post-injection).

COMPARISON: CT abdomen/pelvis pancreatic protocol dated approximately 8 weeks prior (4-cycle restaging); original baseline CT at time of diagnosis.

---

FINDINGS:

PANCREATIC PRIMARY TUMOR:
The hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process now measures approximately 2.6 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 3.0 cm on the 4-cycle restaging scan; 3.4 cm at baseline), representing continued interval decrease in tumor size over the course of neoadjuvant therapy. The margins of the lesion remain ill-defined and spiculated, as is typical of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma both before and after chemotherapy. The lesion is hypovascular relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on the pancreatic parenchymal phase, which remains preserved, though the parenchymal enhancement of the uninvolved pancreatic body and tail appears mildly diminished compared to expected normal, possibly reflecting mild treatment-related or underlying chronic changes. There is mild upstream pancreatic duct prominence within the pancreatic body and tail measuring up to 4 mm, a slight improvement compared to the 5 mm noted on the baseline study, likely reflecting improved biliary/ductal drainage as the mass has decreased in size. No pancreatic duct cutoff sign is identified at the level of the mass.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY (SMA) RELATIONSHIP:
Careful review of the thin-section axial images and curved planar reformats along the SMA demonstrates that the tumor now contacts the right lateral and posterior wall of the SMA over an arc of approximately 120 degrees of circumferential contact (previously estimated at approximately 160 degrees on the 4-cycle restaging scan, and greater than 180 degrees at baseline). There is no imaging evidence of frank encasement. The SMA wall appears smooth without intraluminal narrowing or thrombosis on MIP and multiplanar reformats. There is persistent soft tissue density/stranding immediately adjacent to the SMA in this segment, which is difficult to reliably distinguish from desmoplastic/fibrotic reaction versus residual viable tumor given the imaging characteristics of treated pancreatic adenocarcinoma. No radiographic evidence of arterial stenosis is appreciated.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC VEIN (SMV) AND PORTAL VEIN CONFLUENCE:
The SMV demonstrates near-complete restoration of its normal luminal caliber at the level of the tumor. On the current study, there is only mild soft tissue abutment of the right lateral wall of the SMV without appreciable luminal narrowing, a meaningful improvement compared to the approximately 40% lumen compromise seen at baseline and the mildly persistent narrowing noted at the 4-cycle restaging. The portal vein confluence is well-preserved. No SMV thrombosis or occlusion. No venous collateral formation to suggest hemodynamically significant venous compromise. Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African-descent female with borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma presenting today for post-treatment visit following completion of 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX). She is here for results review, toxicity assessment, tumor board discussion summary, and transition to the next phase of her neoadjuvant treatment plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old retired high school science teacher with a well-characterized diagnosis of moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head/uncinate process, confirmed by EUS-guided fine needle biopsy approximately 4 months ago. At the time of diagnosis, cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass with SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees of circumferential contact and SMV lumen narrowing of approximately 40%, classifying the tumor as borderline resectable by NCCN criteria. PET-CT confirmed locoregional disease without distant metastasis. Molecular profiling (Tempus xT) revealed KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and SMAD4 wild-type (retained on IHC). TMB was low (3 mutations/Mb) and the tumor was microsatellite stable (MSS). No germline actionable variants were identified.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board consensus, the patient was initiated on neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced from standard 180 mg/m2 at initiation given patient age and comorbid diabetes), and 5-FU 2,400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion, administered on a biweekly schedule with G-CSF prophylaxis. The irinotecan dose was further reduced to 120 mg/m2 after cycle 3 due to grade 2 diarrhea. Over the course of 8 cycles, the patient experienced grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 nausea, grade 2 diarrhea, and grade 1 alopecia. She required no hospitalizations and her ECOG performance status remained 1 throughout.

At midpoint restaging after cycle 4, CA 19-9 had fallen from 1,200 U/mL to 380 U/mL and imaging demonstrated minor size reduction of the primary mass (3.4 to 3.0 cm) with some improvement in SMA abutment from >180 to approximately 160 degrees, prompting the decision to complete the full planned 8 cycles.

Today, she has completed all 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. She reports her back pain has significantly improved and is now only intermittently present. Energy levels are slowly returning, though she notes ongoing fatigue that she rates as 4/10 in severity. She continues to experience bilateral fingertip and toe paresthesias consistent with cumulative oxaliplatin-associated neuropathy — she describes these as a persistent "buzzing" sensation, worsened by cold. She has been managing with warm gloves and avoiding cold beverages. Nausea has largely subsided since completing her last cycle approximately 3.5 weeks ago. Appetite has improved modestly; she has regained approximately 2 lbs since the cycle 4 visit. Bowel function is regular (1-2 formed stools daily, no diarrhea). She denies new-onset jaundice, fever, chills, or abdominal distension. She has not noted worsening of her epigastric discomfort. Her blood sugar management remains challenging — she continues on metformin and has required dose adjustments, with fasting glucoses now running in the 160-190 mg/dL range (improvement from 220-280 mg/dL at diagnosis, likely reflecting improved glycemic control with tumor response). HbA1c measured 3 weeks ago was 8.1%.

Restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol and MRI liver were performed after completion of cycle 8 and reviewed at today's visit. The pancreatic head/uncinate mass has decreased to 2.6 cm (from 3.4 cm at baseline, representing approximately a 24% reduction in longest diameter). The SMA abutment has decreased to approximately 120 degrees of circumferential contact (from >180 degrees at diagnosis). The SMV relationship has improved markedly, with only mild abutment and no significant luminal compromise (previously 40% narrowing). MRI liver showed no hepatic metastases. No peritoneal implants or pathologic lymphadenopathy were identified.

CA 19-9 has declined to 85 U/mL from a baseline of 1,200 U/mL — a 93% reduction. CEA was not repeated at this visit but was previously 5.8 ng/mL at diagnosis.

This case was formally re-presented at the multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board this week, attended by HPB surgical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional radiology, pathology, and medical oncology. The surgical oncologist reviewed the restaging imaging in detail. While the tumor has shown a meaningful response to mFOLFIRINOX — both radiographically and by tumor marker — the SMA abutment at 120 degrees remains above the threshold of <90 degrees that would generally be considered technically resectable with low risk of margin-positive resection. The consensus recommendation of the tumor board is to proceed with concurrent chemoradiation therapy for local consolidation, with the primary goal of further reducing SMA abutment and maximizing the probability of achieving a clear surgical margin (R0 resection) at the time of subsequent Whipple procedure. Long-course conventionally fractionated chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine was selected over SBRT-based approaches given the degree of vascular involvement and the desire for prolonged radiation exposure to address the tumor-vessel interface. The patient will be seen in radiation oncology to initiate treatment planning simulation.

The patient has been fully counseled on this plan. She understands the rationale and is agreeable. She has expressed a strong desire to pursue curative-intent treatment and is accepting of additional time on neoadjuvant therapy before surgery. She reports a robust support system at home including her daughter, who accompanies her to most visits.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST — PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old female with a known history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (KRAS G12V mutant, SMAD4 retained, TMB-low, MSS). She was initially treated with 8 cycles of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX followed by concurrent long-course chemoradiation (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions via IMRT with concurrent capecitabine), completed approximately 6 weeks prior to this examination. This study is obtained for restaging prior to surgical planning and multidisciplinary tumor board review.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice multidetector CT scanner using a dedicated pancreatic protocol. Non-contrast, arterial phase (20 seconds post-injection), pancreatic parenchymal phase (40 seconds), and portal venous phase (70 seconds) acquisitions were obtained following intravenous administration of 120 mL of nonionic iodinated contrast (iohexol 350 mg I/mL) at a rate of 4 mL/second. Thin-section axial reconstructions (0.625 mm) were obtained through the pancreatic bed. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats were reviewed. Oral contrast was administered. Images are compared with the prior pancreatic protocol CT obtained after 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX, as well as the baseline pre-treatment study.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS AND PRIMARY TUMOR:
In the region of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, there persists a zone of hypoattenuating soft tissue with poorly marginated borders measuring approximately 2.3 cm in greatest axial dimension, decreased from 2.6 cm on the prior restaging CT (obtained after completion of 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX) and substantially reduced from the baseline measurement of 3.4 cm at initial diagnosis. The conspicuity of the discrete tumor mass is somewhat reduced compared to prior studies, with the previously defined hypodense nodular configuration now appearing more heterogeneous and ill-defined in morphology. This evolution in character likely reflects a combination of residual viable tumor and post-treatment fibrotic and inflammatory changes, and it is not possible on CT imaging alone to reliably distinguish between the two. There is surrounding soft tissue thickening and mild peripancreatic stranding, which is substantially attributed to post-radiation treatment effect. The main pancreatic duct proximal to the lesion continues to demonstrate mild upstream dilatation, measuring approximately 4 mm, slightly improved compared to 5 mm on the baseline study. The uninvolved pancreatic body and tail demonstrate preserved parenchymal attenuation without atrophic change.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
The relationship between the soft tissue mass and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) has changed favorably since baseline. On multiplanar reformatted images, the degree of circumferential soft tissue contact with the SMA is now estimated at approximately 80 to 90 degrees, representing a meaningful improvement compared to the estimated 120 degrees of abutment noted on the post-mFOLFIRINOX restaging CT and substantially reduced from the original abutment exceeding 180 degrees at diagnosis. There is no longer circumferential encasement of the SMA. The fat plane between the soft tissue density and the SMA lateral wall appears at least partially reconstituted along the posterior and right lateral aspects, though some residual soft tissue proximity remains anterolaterally. No gross arterial irregularity or stenosis of the SMA is identified.

With respect to the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), the previously noted abutment with partial luminal narrowing has continued to improve. The SMV now demonstrates only mild abutment along its left lateral wall without convincing luminal compromise or distortion. The vessel retains its rounded caliber without teardrop deformity. The SMV-portal vein confluence appears patent. The portal vein is of normal caliber without evidence of thrombosis or compression. The hepatic artery proper and its right and left branches demonstrate normal enhancement and caliber without encasement or stenosis. The celiac axis appears unremarkable.

No collateral venous formation or cavernous transformation is identified to suggest occult venous obstruction.

The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. Hepatic parenchymal attenuation is homogeneous throughout all phases of enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on this study. No hypodense, arterially enhancing, or washout-demonstrating lesions are seen in any hepatic segment. The common bile duct measures 5 mm in caliber, within normal limits, unchanged from prior study. No bowel obstruction.

LYMPH NODES:

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued favorable evolution of the pancreatic head/uncinate soft tissue density since the initiation of neoadjuvant therapy. The primary lesion now measures approximately 2.3 cm, reduced from 2.6 cm on the prior post-mFOLFIRINOX scan and from the baseline of 3.4 cm, representing a cumulative reduction of approximately 32% in greatest dimension over the full neoadjuvant treatment course. The distal common bile duct measures 3.0 cm in length and 0.5 cm in diameter; the bile duct lumen is patent. The gallbladder is received intact, measures 8.0 x 3.2 cm, and contains dark green bile and no calculi. The gallbladder wall thickness is 0.2 cm; no mucosal lesions are identified.

The specimen is inked and serially sectioned according to the standard Leeds protocol for pancreaticoduodenectomy specimens. The anterior surface is inked blue, posterior/retroperitoneal (uncinate) surface is inked black, SMA margin is inked green, bile duct margin is inked red.

On serial sectioning of the pancreatic head perpendicular to the long axis, there is an ill-defined, tan-white, firm, stellate mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process region measuring 2.1 x 1.8 x 1.5 cm (greatest dimension 2.1 cm). The mass has a gritty texture on cut surface and shows central fibrosis with scattered pale yellow foci suspicious for residual tumor or acellular mucin pools, interspersed with denser white fibrotic areas, consistent with treated carcinoma with treatment response. The mass does not grossly extend to the mucosal surface of the duodenum. The tumor appears to abut the periductal soft tissue adjacent to the main pancreatic duct; the main pancreatic duct measures 0.3 cm in diameter adjacent to the mass and is not grossly obstructed.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Sections of the pancreatic head mass show a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with histologic features of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma demonstrating treatment response. The residual viable tumor consists of irregular glandular structures and small nests of malignant epithelial cells embedded within an extensively desmoplastic stroma. The tumor glands display moderate-to-severe nuclear pleomorphism, enlarged nuclei with irregular nuclear membranes, prominent nucleoli, increased nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, and amphophilic to pale cytoplasm with focal intraluminal mucin production. Mitotic figures are present.

Treatment effect is prominent and widespread. Extensive areas of hypocellular fibrosis, acellular mucin pools lined by degenerative flat epithelium, stromal hyalinization, hemosiderin deposition, and scattered foamy macrophages are identified, all consistent with post-treatment regression. Viable tumor cells are estimated to comprise approximately 40% of the original tumor bed area, with the remaining 60% represented by treatment-related fibrosis, acellular mucin pools, and necrotic debris. This corresponds to a College of American Pathologists (CAP) histologic tumor regression grade of 2 (moderate response; residual cancer with evident tumor regression).

Perineural invasion: Present. Extensive perineural invasion is identified, with multiple small nerve bundles and larger neural structures containing infiltrating carcinomatous glands and single tumor cells. Perineural invasion is identified in the peripancreatic soft tissue adjacent to the uncinate process and in proximity to the SMA margin soft tissue.

Lymphovascular invasion: Present. Lymphovascular invasion is identified in the form of neoplastic emboli within thin-walled vascular spaces in the peripancreatic adipose tissue.

Duodenal wall involvement: Tumor extends into the periduodenal soft tissue but does not breach the duodenal muscularis propria or involve the duodenal mucosa.

Common bile duct: No carcinoma identified in the submitted sections of the common bile duct wall adjacent to the primary tumor. Mild reactive changes in the bile duct epithelium.

Main pancreatic duct: Mild ductal ectasia adjacent to the tumor. No high-grade pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN-3) identified at the margin.

Uninvolved pancreatic parenchyma: Sections show moderate pancreatic lobular atrophy with interlobular fibrosis and mild chronic pancreatitis-like changes, consistent with post-treatment effect and background obstructive pancreatitis. Islets of Langerhans are scattered and reduced in number.

Gallbladder: Sections show mild chronic cholecystitis with Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses. No carcinoma, dysplasia, or adenoma identified.

ADDITIONAL COMMENTS
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. The pathologic findings are consistent with moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with significant but incomplete treatment response (CAP grade 2) following neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) and concurrent cape-based chemoradiation. The reduction in tumor size from 3.4 cm at diagnosis to 2.1 cm at resection (38% reduction by greatest dimension) is consistent with a partial pathologic response.

2. The extensive perineural invasion identified in the resection specimen, including near the SMA margin, is characteristic of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and is a recognized adverse histopathologic feature associated with locoregional recurrence risk.

3. Lymphovascular invasion is present, consistent with the identified nodal metastases.

4. SMAD4/DPC4 retained expression on IHC is consistent with the molecular profile identified on preoperative NGS (SMAD4 wild-type), which may confer a slightly more favorable prognosis compared to SMAD4-deleted tumors.

5. Acellular mucin pools at margins: Although acellular mucin pools approach within 1 mm of the posterior margin, these do not contain viable tumor cells. Per current College of American Pathologists and ISGPS consensus guidelines, acellular mucin pools without viable carcinoma cells are not considered positive margins and do not alter the R0 designation.

6. Correlation with preoperative imaging, clinical history, and prior biopsy results (EUS-FNB, pancreatic head, moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, SMAD4 wild-type by prior NGS) is recommended for multidisciplinary tumor board discussion.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African-descent female presenting for her 4-week post-operative follow-up after pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) performed for borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process. The patient was discharged from the hospital on postoperative day 18 with a surgical drain in place and is now returning for wound assessment, drain management review, and surgical pathology discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a retired high school science teacher with a background of type 2 diabetes mellitus (now further complicated by pancreatogenic endocrine insufficiency), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. She initially presented approximately seven months ago with a 3-month history of epigastric pain radiating to the back, 14-pound weight loss, and worsening glycemic control. Cross-sectional imaging at that time demonstrated a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process with SMA abutment greater than 180 degrees of contact and SMV narrowing with approximately 40% luminal compromise, consistent with borderline resectable disease by NCCN criteria. Biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, p53 aberrant overexpression, SMAD4 retained). CA 19-9 at diagnosis was 1,200 U/mL. Next-generation sequencing demonstrated KRAS G12V mutation, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and RNF43 frameshift; microsatellite stable, TMB-low; no actionable fusions or BRCA/PALB2/ATM alterations.

She was treated with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 reduced to 120 mg/m2 after grade 2 diarrhea, 5-FU 2,400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, every 2 weeks) for 8 cycles over approximately 4 months. Restaging after 8 cycles showed a reduction in tumor size to 2.6 cm with significant CA 19-9 decline to 85 U/mL (93% reduction). SMA abutment decreased to approximately 120 degrees. Given persistent borderline SMA involvement, the multidisciplinary tumor board recommended long-course chemoradiation prior to surgical exploration. She received 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions via IMRT with concurrent capecitabine (825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days), completed over approximately 5.5 weeks. Post-chemoradiation restaging CT demonstrated further tumor reduction to 2.3 cm with SMA abutment estimated at 80-90 degrees, CA 19-9 declined to 52 U/mL.

She subsequently underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with regional lymphadenectomy approximately 8 weeks after completing chemoradiation. Operative findings were favorable: no peritoneal or hepatic surface metastases; intraoperative frozen section of the SMA margin was negative; portal vein/SMV reconstruction was not required. Estimated blood loss was 450 mL. She was transferred to the surgical ICU postoperatively in stable condition.

Postoperative course was complicated by:
- Grade B pancreatic fistula (ISGPF grade B), diagnosed on postoperative day 6 based on elevated drain fluid amylase (greater than 3x upper limit of normal on POD 3), managed conservatively with maintenance of the surgical drain, NPO status, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneously three times daily. The drain remained in place at the time of discharge.
- Grade A delayed gastric emptying, requiring nasogastric tube decompression, resolved by approximately postoperative day 14.
- The patient was discharged on postoperative day 18 with the surgical drain in place and instructions for drain care.

Final surgical pathology from the Whipple specimen demonstrated: moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 2.1 cm (reduced from 3.4 cm at diagnosis), with College of American Pathologists pathologic treatment response grade 2 (moderate response, estimated 40% viable tumor, 60% fibrosis/acellular mucin pools). R0 resection confirmed (closest margin: uncinate/SMA margin at 3 mm). Two of 18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN1). Lymphovascular invasion present; extensive perineural invasion present. Pathologic stage: pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition).

She now presents at 4 weeks post-operatively for follow-up. She reports that she has been managing the surgical drain at home with assistance from her daughter. Over the past week, drain output has been decreasing substantially in both volume and character (less turbid, less amber-colored). She continues to have mild incisional discomfort at the midline abdominal wound, which she rates 2/10 and is managed with acetaminophen. She reports a gradual improvement in appetite and has been tolerating a low-fat, small frequent meal diet. She has had no fevers, chills, or rigors at home. She denies nausea or vomiting. She continues to have loose stools (2-3 per day), which is expected given her anatomy post-Whipple. She has been compliant with pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (pancrelipase; PERT) with meals and snacks, though she notes that stool consistency has not fully normalized. Blood glucose monitoring at home has shown values ranging from 140-220 mg/dL fasting; she is currently on insulin (regimen adjusted by endocrinology), and her glucose control remains a work in progress.

She denies worsening abdominal pain, increased drain output, erythema around the drain or wound, signs of wound dehiscence, jaundice, dark urine, clay-colored stool, or new neurologic symptoms. She has not required any emergency department visits since discharge.

Weight at discharge: she had lost approximately 4 lbs during chemoradiation and had additional postoperative weight loss; overall, she estimates she is down approximately 18 lbs from her pre-diagnosis baseline. She has been taking oral nutritional supplements (Ensure Plus) between meals.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A deleted; MSS; TMB-low) who has completed a full course of multimodal neoadjuvant therapy (8 cycles mFOLFIRINOX, then capecitabine-based chemoradiation 50.4 Gy/28 fractions) followed by pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with R0 resection (pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB; 2/18 nodes positive; CAP treatment response grade 2). She is now 4 weeks post-operatively and recovering well overall, with her most notable ongoing issue being a resolving grade B pancreatic fistula.

1. PANCREATIC FISTULA (ISGPF GRADE B) — RESOLVING
   - Drain fluid amylase today is 128 U/L, markedly decreased from the post-operative peak (>3,000 U/L on POD 3) and now approaching normal serum range.
   - Drain output over the past 24 hours is approximately 25 mL of thin, non-turbid fluid — a significant reduction from prior weeks.
   - Clinical status is reassuring: no fever, no abdominal pain beyond baseline surgical tenderness, no signs of sepsis or collection formation.
   - Plan: Given drain amylase normalization and markedly reduced output, the surgical drain will be removed at today's visit. The patient and her daughter are counseled that if she develops fever, increased abdominal pain, or change in clinical status, she should contact the office or report to the emergency department for evaluation.
   - Octreotide: Given resolution of fistula evidenced by drain amylase normalization, octreotide will be discontinued today. No taper required given short-course use.
   - She will follow up with drain site wound care instructions.

2. SURGICAL PATHOLOGY DISCUSSION
   - Formal results reviewed in detail with the patient and her daughter. Key points communicated:
     - R0 (clear margin) resection was achieved — the tumor was completely removed with the closest margin being 3 mm at the SMA/uncinate margin. This is the best possible surgical outcome in this setting.
     - Pathologic treatment response (CAP grade 2) indicates meaningful but incomplete tumor kill from neoadjuvant therapy — approximately 40% viable tumor remained. This represents a moderate response, consistent with the clinical and radiographic improvements observed.
     - Two of 18 lymph nodes were found to harbor microscopic metastatic disease (pN1) — this upgrades the final pathologic stage to IIB, which carries prognostic significance but does not preclude curative intent.
     - Extensive perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion are adverse histologic features that highlight the importance of adjuvant therapy.
   - The patient was given time to process this information and asked thoughtful questions about what the node-positive result means for her long-term prognosis. We discussed that Stage IIB pancreatic cancer after R0 resection represents a meaningful opportunity for cure, particularly given the degree of treatment response observed, but that approximately 50-70% of patients experience recurrence within 2 years and that adjuvant chemotherapy is a critical next step. She expressed understanding and commitment to proceeding with adjuvant therapy.

3. PLANNING FOR ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY
   - Adjuvant chemotherapy is strongly recommended given Stage IIB disease (pN1), extensive perineural invasion, and lymphovascular invasion. The standard of care in this context (and supported by the ESPAC-4 trial) is gemcitabine plus capecitabine for 6 cycles (approximately 6 months).
   - She has received prior FOLFIRINOX-based therapy (neoadjuvant) and capecitabine-based chemoradiation. Gemcitabine plus capecitabine (gem/cape) represents an appropriate adjuvant regimen with a well-characterized toxicity profile.
   - Adjuvant chemotherapy should begin no earlier than 8-10 weeks post-operatively to allow adequate surgical recovery. Given the pancreatic fistula, her performance status, nutritional status, and drain removal today, I anticipate she will be ready to initiate adjuvant therapy in approximately 8-10 weeks (pending surgical recovery and nutritional optimization). I will coordinate closely with her medical oncologist for this transition.
   - I have communicated with her medical oncologist today regarding the plan and timing.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 66-year-old female with recently resected pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head/uncinate process (pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB). Status post pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 10 weeks prior, complicated by grade B pancreatic fistula now resolved. The previously described grade B pancreatic fistula — for which a surgical drain was maintained postoperatively — has resolved; the drain tract has closed and there is no residual peripancreatic collection. The superior mesenteric artery and superior mesenteric vein remain patent and widely opacified without hemodynamically significant stenosis or occlusion. CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC, THIN CUTS)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
A 66-year-old woman with a 3-month history of progressive epigastric pain radiating to the back, 14-pound unintentional weight loss, new-onset worsening of previously controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (fasting glucoses 220-280 mg/dL despite metformin adjustment), early satiety, and intermittent nausea. Markedly elevated CA 19-9 at 1,200 U/mL and mild transaminase elevation. Evaluate for pancreatic mass or other upper abdominal pathology.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice multidetector CT scanner following the administration of 120 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (iohexol, 350 mgI/mL) at a rate of 4 mL/sec via right antecubital vein. Acquisitions obtained in the non-contrast, late arterial/pancreatic parenchymal phase (approximately 40 seconds), portal venous phase (approximately 70 seconds), and delayed phase (approximately 180 seconds). 3 mm axial reconstructions were obtained through the abdomen and pelvis; 1.5 mm thin-cut reconstructions through the pancreatic bed were reformatted in the axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Maximum intensity projection (MIP) and curved planar reformations of the superior mesenteric artery and vein were generated at the workstation.

Oral contrast was not administered to optimize vascular and pancreatic parenchymal assessment.

Comparison: No prior abdominal CT available for comparison.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is a hypoattenuating, poorly marginated mass centered in the pancreatic head and uncinate process measuring approximately 3.4 cm in greatest axial dimension (craniocaudal dimension approximately 3.1 cm, transverse approximately 3.4 cm). The mass is conspicuous on the pancreatic parenchymal phase, demonstrating markedly reduced enhancement relative to the adjacent normal pancreatic parenchyma, which enhances briskly in the arterial phase. The margins of the mass are ill-defined and spiculated, without a definable capsule. There is associated mild upstream dilatation of the main pancreatic duct, which measures up to 5 mm in the body and tail (within or at the upper limit of normal). The pancreatic body and tail parenchyma appear mildly atrophic, likely reflecting obstructive ductal changes. No discrete pancreatic cysts are identified. No pancreatic calcifications. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The common bile duct measures 6 mm in its mid-portion, within normal limits.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY (SMA):
The mass encases and directly contacts the superior mesenteric artery over a craniocaudal segment of approximately 1.8 cm. Careful assessment on coronal and axial thin-cut reconstructions demonstrates tumor-vessel interface extending greater than 180 degrees around the circumference of the SMA at its point of maximal contact. The SMA lumen appears patent with no stenosis; there is no evidence of intraluminal thrombus. No periarticular fat plane is preserved at the site of abutment.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC VEIN (SMV) AND PORTAL VENOUS CONFLUENCE:
The mass abuts the superior mesenteric vein along an approximately 2.1 cm segment. There is visible narrowing of the SMV lumen at the point of greatest contact, estimated at approximately 40% luminal reduction compared to the adjacent uninvolved vessel caliber. No occlusion or thrombosis of the SMV is identified. The narrowing is smooth and extrinsic in character, consistent with tumor abutment rather than direct invasion with intraluminal extension. The SMV is patent above and below the zone of contact. The portal vein confluence and main portal vein are uninvolved with a preserved fat plane. No cavernous transformation of the portal vein.

CELIAC AXIS AND HEPATIC ARTERY:
The celiac axis arises normally from the aorta and is uninvolved by tumor. A preserved fat plane is maintained between the mass and the celiac trunk. The common hepatic artery and proper hepatic artery course normally. No tumor contact with or encasement of the hepatic artery. The gastroduodenal artery origin is not clearly visualized in the region of the mass but no definitive involvement is identified.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on any phase of contrast enhancement. No biliary ductal dilatation. No portal hypertension changes. No hepatic steatosis.

GALLBLADDER AND BILE DUCTS:
The gallbladder is present, normal in size, with no cholelithiasis, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The extrahepatic and intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated, as noted above. No biliary obstruction.

SPLEEN:
Normal in size at 10.2 cm in longest dimension. Homogeneous enhancement. No focal splenic lesions. The splenic vein is patent.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal nodules or masses.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys enhance symmetrically and excrete contrast normally. No hydronephrosis. No solid renal masses. A 5 mm simple cortical cyst is noted in the right kidney, benign in appearance. No urolithiasis.

STOMACH AND DUODENUM:
The stomach is not distended; the gastric wall appears normal. The duodenum is difficult to evaluate given the abutting mass in the pancreatic head, but no definitive mucosal irregularity or luminal obstruction is identified. The duodenal sweep appears mildly displaced by the pancreatic head mass.

SMALL BOWEL AND COLON:
The visualized small bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness without obstruction. The colon is unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the peripancreatic, periportal, celiac, para-aortic, or mesenteric distributions. A few small nodes are noted adjacent to the porta hepatis and in the mesenteric root, the largest measuring up to 9 mm in short axis, which falls below threshold for radiographic suspicion. No confluent nodal masses.

PERITONEUM AND ASCITES:
No peritoneal implants or omental caking. No ascites. The peritoneal surfaces appear smooth and unremarkable on all phases.

VASCULATURE:
The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber without aneurysmal dilatation. The inferior vena cava and iliac vessels are patent. Atherosclerotic calcifications are seen in the infrarenal aorta and common iliac arteries, mild in degree.

BONY STRUCTURES:
No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions suspicious for metastasis. Mild degenerative changes in the lumbar spine.

SUBDIAPHRAGMATIC LUNG BASES:
Small bilateral posterior lung base atelectasis. No pleural effusions. No discrete lung nodules at the lung bases.

IMPRESSION:

1. PANCREATIC HEAD/UNCINATE MASS: A 3.4 cm hypoattenuating, poorly marginated mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process is highly suspicious for primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma in this clinical context. Tissue sampling is recommended for histopathologic confirmation.

2. VASCULAR INVOLVEMENT — BORDERLINE RESECTABLE PATTERN:
   a. Superior mesenteric artery (SMA): The tumor contacts the SMA over a length of approximately 1.8 cm with an estimated arc of encasement exceeding 180 degrees of the vessel circumference at the point of maximal involvement. No periarticular fat plane is preserved at this site.
   b. Superior mesenteric vein (SMV): The mass abuts the SMV over approximately 2.1 cm with approximately 40% luminal narrowing in this segment; the vein remains patent without occlusion. The portal vein confluence is uninvolved.
   c. Celiac axis and hepatic artery: Not involved. Fat planes maintained.
   These findings collectively are consistent with a borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma per NCCN anatomic criteria, primarily driven by the degree of SMA abutment and SMV narrowing.

3. NO DISTANT METASTASIS: No liver metastases, peritoneal implants, or pathologically suspicious lymphadenopathy identified on this examination. No ascites.

4. Mild upstream main pancreatic duct dilatation (up to 5 mm) with mild parenchymal atrophy in the body/tail, consistent with obstructive changes from the head mass.

5. No biliary ductal obstruction at this time, though the common bile duct is at the upper limit of normal. Continued monitoring warranted given location of mass.

This study was reviewed and interpreted by the attending abdominal radiologist. A verbal preliminary report was communicated to the referring clinician at the time of interpretation. This case has been flagged for multidisciplinary pancreatic oncology and surgical oncology review.
